titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the W. S. Lewis Collection of George Townshend Correspondence

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday October 26, 2022 at 01:25

did.unittitle: W. S. Lewis Collection of George Townshend Correspondence
did.abstract: The W. S. Lewis Collection of George Townshend Correspondence holds approximately 350 letters sent to and by George Townshend primarily during his tenure as Lord Lieutenant of Ireland in the years 1767 through 1772. The bulk of the collection is held in Series I, where most of the letters concern military and political topics, with some more personal and family correspondence. Many of the writers were appealing to Townshend for patronage appointments in regiments, bishoprics, and deaneries, for positions on local boards, and for peerage titles and honors. Very often they were soliciting on behalf of their sons, brothers, nephews, or protégés, as well as for family members of friends. Letters also discuss pensions and pensioners, and usually express fawning gratitude for the many unspecified kindnesses that Lord Townshend had previously bestowed upon the writers. Series II holds eight letters received by three family members: George Townshend's wife Charlotte (-1770) and son John (1757-1833), and his grandfather Charles Townshend (1674-1738).

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. George Townshend Letters, 1736-1800. II. Other Townshend Family Letters, 1708-1790.

bioghist.head: George Townshend (1724–1807)
bioghist.p: George Townshend, a British politician and caricaturist, was born in London on February 28, 1724, to Charles Townshend, third Viscount Townshend (1700-1764) and his wife Etheldreda Harrison Townshend (1708-1788). He attended Eton College and St John's College, Cambridge (MA 1749). Following a brief military career, where he fought in at the Battle of Culloden and served as aide-de-camp to Prince William Augustus, Duke of Cumberland, Townshend left the army with the title of lieutenant colonel. While in Europe he was elected to Parliament at MP for Norfolk (1747) where he was a political ally of William Pitt, the Duke of Newcastle, and the Earl of Bute. As an MP he was responsible for the passage of the Militia Act of 1757.

In 1758, during the Seven Years' War, Townshend was promoted to the rank of colonel, was given command of the 64th Regiment of the Footguards and served in Quebec under General James Wolfe. He was promoted to major general in 1761 and served in Germany (1761) and in Portugal (1762) during the Spanish invasion. In 1763 he became Lieutenant General of the Ordnance, and on August 19, 1767, Lord Lieutenant of Ireland. Once there, an important assignment was to expand the size of the British army in Ireland by filling Irish regiments. Townshend remained in that office until 1772 when he returned to the Ordnance. He continued to achieve higher ranks in the military as he was appointed colonel in 1773, general in 1782, and field marshal in 1796.

George Townshend left the House of Commons in 1764 when, upon the death of his father, he became fourth Viscount Townshend; he was created Marquess Townshend of Raynham in 1786. He was twice married, in 1751 to Charlotte Compton, Baroness Ferrers (died 1770 at Leixlip Castle, County Kildare), with whom he had six children, and in 1773, to Anne Montgomery (died 1819), with whom he had seven children. Townshend died at his home, Raynham Hall, on September 14, 1807, and was buried in St Mary Churchyard in East Raynham, Norfolk.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: The material in the collection came from two acquisitions made by Wilmarth Lewis: one in September 1933 from the dealer G. H. Last of Bromley, Kent, and another in June 1964 from Maggs Bros. The more than 250 letters and documents in the former group comprises the bulk of the collection. James Cuninghame Durham told Lewis (LWL MSS 20) that the letters had been discovered at Raynham Hall in Norfolk, after the death of John Villiers Stuart Townshend (1831–1899), 5th Marquess Townshend. The second group of 90 letters was acquired by Maggs from a Sotheby's sale on May 25-27, 1964 (lot 551), and no further provenance information is known. Readers may consult the library's collection file for LWL MSS 38 for more information regarding the contents of the two acquisitions.
In addition, a single letter from John Burgoyne was a gift to Wilmarth Lewis from Walter Kennedy, made in memory of Thomas Oakes, on October 19th, 1972.
Prior to February 2022 material now in LWL MSS 38 was interfiled in the Lewis Walpole Library Manuscript Miscellany (LWL MSS MISC).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: W. S. Lewis Collection of George Townshend Correspondence. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Typed transcriptions were made by library staff for most of the letters in the collection. In 2022 the transcripts were photocopied on acid-free paper and the originals discarded.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The W. S. Lewis Collection of George Townshend Correspondence holds approximately 350 letters sent to and by George Townshend primarily during his tenure as Lord Lieutenant of Ireland in the years 1767 through 1772. The bulk of the collection is held in Series I, where most of the letters concern military and political topics, with some more personal and family correspondence. Many of the writers were appealing to Townshend for patronage appointments in regiments, bishoprics, and deaneries, for positions on local boards, and for peerage titles and honors. Very often they were soliciting on behalf of their sons, brothers, nephews, or protégés, as well as for family members of friends. Letters also discuss pensions and pensioners, and usually express fawning gratitude for the many unspecified kindnesses that Lord Townshend had previously bestowed upon the writers. Series II holds eight letters received by three family members: George Townshend's wife Charlotte (-1770) and son John (1757-1833), and his grandfather Charles Townshend (1674-1738).

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The W. S. Lewis Collection of George Townshend Correspondence is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

persname.part: Hertford, Francis Seymour Conway, Marquess of, 1719-1794

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into three subseries: Incoming Letters, Outgoing Letters, and Other Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds the bulk of the collection. While the correspondents mainly wrote from England and Ireland about positions in those countries some letters cast a wider scope: Joseph Gorham sought positions in Tobago, Dominique, and Florida, and William Kelly wrote from New York in November 1767 to say he is sending "curious seeds and plants," in addition to barrels of apples and cranberries to Townshend in London. Sir George Colebrooke wrote for the directors of the East India Company regarding Townshend's help in recruiting officers "among the Catholics of Ireland," and Simon Fraser wrote from Chaves, Portugal, in 1771 about the political situation there. However, local concerns are predominant: William O'Brien, Earl of Inchiquin, wrote over several years to recommend men for sheriff of County Clare, where he was the governor and keeper of the rolls; Charles Coote, Earl of Bellomont, sent in his nomination to fill the job of state trumpeter; and Sir Robert Wilmot, who served as Townshend's private secretary, wrote seven letters discussing pensioners and other government business. John Perceval, Earl of Egmont, wrote lengthily and passionately about the augmentation of the Irish regiments and the political scene in Ireland; among his six letters is one written from London on September 15, 1768, which runs to eight pages and gives his views of the Irish people and his "most melancholy and almost desponding reflections upon the state of our affairs." Some of the more unusual items in the series are two letters from the Irish landscape painter Jonathan Fisher describing his search around London for engravers to reproduce his works, a small wash portrait that Turner Calvert enclosed of himself to remind Townshend that they had met and that he too is connected to a good Norfolk family, and a single letter from the English army officer, military engineer, and surveyor Hugh Debbieg who had, like Townshend, served in Quebec during the Seven Years' War.

Among the correspondents more heavily represented in the collection are James Francis Erskine, whose thirteen letters including five written in 1769 with first-hand coverage of John Wilkes' expulsion Parliament; Lord Lifford's thirty-five letters cover both business and personal affairs, most pointedly a heartfelt letter written after the death of Townshend's first wife Charlotte at Leixlip Castle on September 14, 1770; several of John Hely-Hutchinson's seventeen letters are written about Charlotte Townshend's decline and death. Francis Seymour Conway sent eight letters on the topics of military matters and news of his family, including his son in the Dragoons and his daughter Anne Moore, Lady Drogheda. There are letters that touch on personal or purely social themes such as those from Edward King and Thomas Thoroton on the topic of hounds and hunting, and from a few women such as Carolina Harrington writing to introduce an acquaintance who was traveling to Ireland "to abjure in form the Roman Catholick persuasion which he has for some time disapproved." Also present are letters from the Archbishop of York, the Bishops of Kilmore, Limerick, Norwich, and Waterford, and the Deans of Ferns, Killaloe, and Norwich, and four letters from members of the Walpole family: Sir Edward Walpole, Horatio Walpole, Baron Walpole (1678-1757), Horatio Walpole (1723–1809), and Robert Walpole of Athlone.
The second subseries, Outgoing Letters, consists of drafts of nine folders of letters Townshend wrote to family and colleagues, most of whom have letters in the first subseries Outgoing Letters.

The third subseries, Other Papers, consists of three documents: the Freedom of the County of the City of Waterford awarded to His Excellency George Lord Viscount Townshend, as Lord Lieutenant General and General Governor of Ireland in 1768, and two petitions on behalf of Thomas Dawson submitted by a committee of magistrates of Yarmouth to Townshend and his brother Charles.

did.unittitle: Buckingham, George Nugent Temple Grenville, Marquess of, 1753-1813

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A facsimile of an ink portrait of Calvert was laid in, and described in, the letter. The drawing was removed and separately cataloged in 2022 (LWL Min 152).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Encloses a memorial from the Earl of Cavan to Lord Townshend, asking for an increase in his pension as a provision to support his daughter Lady Gertruce Lambart.

did.unittitle: Clanricarde, John Smith De Burgh, Earl of, 1720-1782

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Clarendon was styled Baron Hyde from 1756 to 1776.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Dashwood was created Baron le Despencer in 1763, and signed his letter with that title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Draper's letter of August 22, 1767, encloses a letter from Cadwallader Blayney to Draper dated August 11, 1767.

did.unittitle: Grafton, Augustus Henry Fitzroy, Duke of, 1735-1811

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Grafton's letter of August 4, 1771, enclosed an appeal from Thomas Cosby for promotion to lieutenant in the 49th Regiment.

did.unittitle: Hertford, Francis Seymour Conway, Marquess of, 1719-1794
Home, Alexander Home, Earl of, approximately 1715-1786
Howth, Thomas St. Lawrence, Earl of, 1730-1801
Inchiquin, William O'Brien, Earl of, approximately 1700-1777
Kinsale, John de Courcy, Lord, approximately 1717-1776
Leeds, George William Frederick Osborne, Duke of, 1775-1856

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: James Hewitt was a British politician who served as Lord Chancellor of Ireland from 1767 to 1789.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also present is a petition sent on behalf of John Ferguson, asking for the government to provide protection to him and his house; MacDonnell was at the time High Sherif of Antrim.

did.unittitle: Montagu, George Brudenell Montagu, Duke of, 1712-1790
Mornington, Garrett Colley Wellesley, Earl of, 1735-1781
Portland, William Henry Cavendish-Bentinck, Duke of, 1738-1809

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also present is a letter from Thoroton to Colonel Frazer, dated July 26, 1769, introducing Ensign Hudson, the bearer of the letter.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: George Townshend (1755-1811) was the eldest son of George Townshend and his first wife Charlotte Compton, Baroness Ferrers and Compton (-1770). Upon the death of his mother he inherited the title Baron de Ferrars, and in 1784 he was created Earl of Leicester. A politician and an antiquarian with a particular interest in heraldry and archaeology, Townshend was admitted to the Royal Society in 1781 and was made a trustee of the British Museum in 1787. He was president of the Society of Antiquaries from 1784 until his death.

did.unittitle: Montagu, George Brudenell Montagu, Duke of, 1712-1790

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Thomas Townshend was the uncle of George Townshend

did.unittitle: Freedom of the County of the City of Waterford

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A parchment document with red wax Seal of Mayorality, signed by Waterford's Mayor William Price.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two copies of a petition, signed by fourteen men, submitted to Charles and George Townshend on behalf of Thomas Dawson.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into three subseries: To Charles Townshend, To Charlotte Townshend, and To John Townshend.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains eight letters received by the grandfather, wife, and son of George Townshend.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Charles Townshend, second Viscount Townshend (1674-1738), was the grandfather of George Townshend; he married Dorothy Walpole (1686-1726), sister of Robert Walpole, in 1713.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A letter, signed "J. Drumd.," possibly a British merchant and agent for the government at Amsterdam, discussing financial concerns. It is addressed on the verso to The Right Honourable Lord Viscount Townshend ambassador Extraordinary & Plenipotentiary from the Queen of Great Britain at The Hague.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Lady Charlotte Compton, the first wife of George Townshend, was the second daughter of James Compton, Earl of Northampton, and his wife, Elizabeth Shirley, Baroness Ferrers; she married Townshend in 1751.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John Townshend was the second son born to George and Charlotte Townshend.
